data.title: China’s Anta considers bid for German sportswear rival Puma amid 62% stock slump: sources
data.description:
Chinese sports apparel company Anta Sports Products is among firms exploring a potential takeover of Puma, according to people familiar with the matter.
Hong Kong-listed Anta has been working with an adviser to evaluate a bid for Puma, said the people who asked not to be identified because the information was private. The company might team up with a private equity firm if it decided to move forward with an offer, some of the people said.
Other potential bidders could include rival Chinese apparel firm Li Ning, the people said. Li Ning, named after the legendary gymnast who founded the company, has been discussing financing options with banks as it takes an early look at Puma, the people said. Puma may also attract interest from sportswear companies such as Japan’s Asics, the people said.
Deliberations were preliminary and it was unclear which suitors would proceed with bids, the people said. The valuation expectations of Puma’s biggest shareholder, France’s billionaire Pinault family, may represent a major hurdle to any transaction, the people said. Puma shares have dropped 62 per cent in Frankfurt this year, giving the company a market value of €2.5 billion (US$2.9 billion).

The Pinault family’s Artemis holding company owned 29 per cent of Puma at the end of last year, according to the firm’s annual report.
Anta – which owns brands including Fila and Jack Wolfskin – has gained 9 per cent in Hong Kong this year, giving the company a market value of nearly US$31 billion. An Anta-led consortium, which also included Asian buyout firm FountainVest Partners, paid US$5.2 billion in 2019 to acquire Amer Sports, the owner of brands like Salomon and Arc’teryx.
Li Ning’s stock has risen 7 per cent this year in Hong Kong, giving it a market value of almost US$6 billion.
A representative for Anta did not respond to requests for comment, while representatives for Artemis, Asics and Puma declined to comment.
Responding to a Bloomberg News query, Li Ning said in a statement that it remained focused on the growth of its brand, and had not conducted any “substantive” negotiations or evaluations relating to Puma.
Francois-Henri Pinault, managing partner at Artemis, said in September that the Puma stake was “interesting” but “isn’t strategic”, and that options were being kept open regarding the holding.
Puma has been trying to revamp itself under new CEO Arthur Hoeld after failing to generate much enthusiasm for its products with consumers in recent years. The German firm in July appointed ex-Adidas executive Andreas Hubert as chief operating officer. Hubert is a 20-year veteran of Adidas who served for the past four years as the company’s chief information officer.

data.title: Can mediators stop Afghan-Pakistani war as ‘cat-and-mouse game’ enters retaliatory phase?
data.description:
A ceasefire between Afghanistan’s Taliban regime and Pakistan, agreed after intense clashes last month, is hanging by a diplomatic thread, following a wave of suicide bombings in Pakistan and disputed claims that it retaliated with cross-border drone strikes on Tuesday.
With both countries vowing vengeance, analysts say regional states acting as mediators – Qatar and Turkey, and Iran and Russia – have only a narrow window of opportunity to prevent another round of hostilities.
Otherwise, tensions between the South Asian neighbours could degenerate into prolonged proxy warfare, creating space for transnational terrorist groups based there, particularly the Islamic State-Khorasan (Isis-K), to regenerate and plot overseas attacks.
According to Omar Samad, a former Afghan ambassador to Canada and France, the “dangerous cat-and-mouse game” between Islamabad and Kabul has entered “the advanced retaliation stage”. Tensions could easily escalate before mediators had time to react or intervene, he told This Week in Asia.

On the other hand, either or both Islamabad and Kabul could be “using the new escalation to prod and enable mediation”, said Samad, currently a senior fellow at the Atlantic Council, a Washington think tank.
Kabul has not yet acted on its threat of “a necessary response [to] be taken at the proper time” for the air strikes it alleges Islamabad carried out on targets in three eastern Afghan provinces – Khost, Kunar and Paktika – where several thousand Tehreek-i-Taliban Pakistan (TTP) insurgents are encamped, according to UN assessments.
“These hostile actions by Pakistani forces achieve nothing,” Afghan government spokesman Zabihullah Mujahid said in a social media post on Tuesday. “They only prove that operations driven by flawed intelligence inflame tensions and expose the ongoing failures of Pakistan’s military regime.”
After each of the three TTP suicide bombing attacks in Pakistan this month, Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif promised to hunt down the Afghanistan-based plotters. Each attack could easily have become mass-casualty incidents, had the terrorists not been thwarted by security forces.


But Islamabad has strongly denied Kabul’s claims that it conducted air strikes against targets in Afghanistan on Tuesday. “Whenever Pakistan attacks someone, it announces it,” Pakistan’s chief military spokesman Lieutenant General Ahmed Sharif Chaudhry told reporters in Islamabad.
“It appears the ceasefire is now being honoured in the breach,” said Michael Kugelman, senior South Asia fellow at the Asia-Pacific Foundation of Canada.
Pakistan’s denial of carrying out fresh strikes “may be meant to protect the integrity of the truce”, he told This Week in Asia. “But the strike and likely retaliations to come will make it all the more difficult to hold”.
Regional help
For the time being, Pakistan appears to prefer engaging with the regional neighbours working to avert the collapse of the shaky ceasefire agreed with Afghanistan in October under mediation by Qatar and Turkey.
Defence Minister Khawaja Mohammed Asif, in a television appearance on Tuesday, said he expected regional states to intervene to prevent a war.
“It won’t take long,” Asif said. “Otherwise, they [the Taliban regime] will be isolated and their total isolation will ultimately result in a collapse.”
Turkey and Qatar are reportedly preparing to co-host a third round of talks in Istanbul over the establishment of a ceasefire monitoring mechanism, following two failed attempts to achieve a breakthrough earlier this month.

A separate initiative launched last week by Iran and Russia was boosted by the timely arrival of Iran’s National Security Adviser Ali Larijani in Islamabad on Monday, as the violence and rhetoric escalated.
With neither side in any mood to budge, analysts fear Pakistan could seek to covertly support anti-Taliban regime Afghan groups in retaliation for Kabul’s refusal to stop cross-border TTP attacks.
“It would be tempting for the military brass in Pakistan to use … rogue anti-Taliban elements looking for a sponsor as a nuisance or a proxy to add to the pressures,” Samad said.
But if Pakistan “formally turns on the Taliban” and starts creating and backing armed anti-Taliban groups, Kugelman said that could lead to “internal conflict risks and the type of conditions that terror groups thrive on”.
Instability creates opportunity
Already, Isis-K is taking advantage of the instability created by the current conflict, according to terrorism expert Lucas Webber.
Cross-border clashes, mutual accusations over safe havens, and disrupted security cooperation have weakened the states’ control in the borderlands, giving Isis-K “more space to move fighters, seek refuge and reorganise while continuing to target both countries”, said Webber, a senior threat intelligence analyst at Tech Against Terrorism, a UN-supported advisory.
He said renewed turbulence and shifting security priorities could also give the terror group opportunities to adapt and recover parts of its propaganda infrastructure, following a spate of arrests in the aftermath of Isis-K attacks in Iran, Russia and Turkey early last year, as well as foiled plots in Europe.
As Kabul concentrates greater military and intelligence resources on managing the crisis with Islamabad, Webber said Isis-K might “find space to resume planning and enabling external operations”, including attacks against international targets and foreign nationals in Afghanistan and Pakistan.
Despite such ramifications, neither side “feels weak or strong enough, emboldened or disheartened enough to blink [first] and rely on diplomacy as the only option left”, former Afghan diplomat Samad said.
“Whether theirs is an ulterior strategic motive that is not apparent yet”, he said. “That could range from regime change in Afghanistan to great power rivalries and even prodding Pakistani vulnerabilities.”

According to former senior White House adviser on South Asia Joshua T. White, Pakistani leaders will increasingly have to deal with “the intense public pressure that is generated from TTP attacks on Pakistani civilians”.
In contrast, Afghanistan’s Taliban leadership, “by choice and circumstance”, is relatively less vulnerable to public pressure.
“The challenge with this conflict is that the Taliban government in Afghanistan simply does not respond to the same threats and inducements that would be ordinarily compelling for other governments,” said White, now a professor of international affairs at the Johns Hopkins School of Advanced International Studies in Washington.
According to Kugelman, “the best we can hope for” is that Afghanistan and Pakistan agree to respect the ceasefire.
“This wouldn’t address the root cause of the crisis, but at least it would keep violence down and limit the risk of wider destabilisation”, he said.

data.title: Chinese plan to spur consumption aims for 3 sectors each worth US$141 billion
data.description:
China has launched a 19-point plan to better align the supply of consumer goods with demand, as Beijing steps up its efforts to rebalance its economy towards a consumption-driven growth model.
A document outlining the plan that was issued by the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology and five other departments on Wednesday laid out goals that included the cultivation of three consumption sectors each worth over 1 trillion yuan (US$141.2 billion) by 2027, as well as 10 hotspots worth 100 billion yuan each.
The trillion-yuan sectors included products for the elderly and consumer electronics, while the consumption hotspots included fitness equipment, pet food and supplies, civilian drones and trendy toys, Vice-Minister of Industry and Information Technology Xie Yuansheng said at a news conference in Beijing on Thursday.
“These areas have demonstrated strong growth momentum and enormous development potential, giving the consumer market important characteristics such as diversification and vitality,” he said.
The plan singled out a range of specific sectors to promote, doubling down on areas Beijing has previously identified as key growth drivers.
For instance, in September it announced ambitions to drive consumption by building a 7 trillion yuan sports sector by 2030 through measures such as promoting more sports events and expanding the supply of sports products.


Similarly, a “silver economy” has emerged as the country grapples with a rapidly ageing population. The plan calls for the development of “urgently needed products” such as elderly care service robots, multifunctional nursing beds, and health monitoring equipment.
Xie said the current quantity and quality of elderly care products were insufficient to meet demand.
“Promoting overall supply-demand alignment can unleash consumption potential to the greatest extent,” he said, emphasising that policies should encourage enterprises to produce goods the public wanted.
Authorities vowed to promote flexible and customised manufacturing models that can efficiently respond to shifting consumer demands, as well as the use of artificial intelligence across the consumer goods industry.
The plan also calls for the development of rising business models, such as live streaming and second-hand e-commerce, alongside “sharing economy” services ranging from shared bicycles to wheelchairs.
It said the “contribution rate of consumption to economic growth will steadily increase” by 2030.
China, the world’s second-largest economy, has placed consumption at the centre of its agenda to counter deflationary pressures amid heightened trade tensions.
Last year, consumption contributed 44.5 per cent of China’s economic growth and accounted for 56.6 per cent of its gross domestic product – significantly less than in many major economies around the world.
A prolonged property crisis and weak wage growth have weighed heavily on Chinese consumer sentiment, curbing people’s appetite for spending.
While the country’s trade-in programme for appliances and other goods has been a major driver of spending this year, its effects have faded since the initial boost passed its peak.
Retail sales growth has continued to decelerate in recent months. It rose by 2.9 per cent year on year last month, after growing by 3 per cent in September.

data.title: Hong Kong developers Sino Group, NWD delay home sales after devastating Tai Po fire
data.description:
Hong Kong’s developers have postponed property sales scheduled for Friday as the city mourns the deaths of dozens of residents in a devastating fire that broke out in Tai Po.
Sino Group and New World Development announced on Thursday that the sale of 150 units at One Park Place in Yau Tong, and 63 units at the Austin Bohemian project in Tsim Sha Tsui, respectively, would be deferred.
Both Sino Group and NWD did not disclose the reason for the postponement or when the sales would be rescheduled.
The announcements came a day after a massive blaze broke out in seven of eight buildings at Wang Fuk Court in the northern New Territories.
The fire was still raging as of Thursday morning, according to authorities. At least 44 people, including a firefighter, died, with nearly 280 people still missing. Among those hospitalised, 45 were in serious condition. Some 900 people sought refuge in various community halls.
The cancellation of the property sales was one of a number of activities suspended.
Political parties suspended campaigning for the December 7 Legislative Council election, with Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu saying the government had halted all publicity efforts as the administration was prioritising disaster relief.
The 2025 Oxfam Trailwalker race on Friday has reportedly been cancelled by organisers. Official confirmation is expected soon.
The Wang Fuk Court fire is one of the worst in Hong Kong’s history, with casualties exceeding the Garley Building fire in Jordan in 1996, when 41 people died.

data.title: 11 killed, 2 hurt after test train crashes into workers in southwestern China
data.description:
A test train crashed into railway workers in China’s southwestern Yunnan province early on Thursday morning, killing 11 people and injuring two, state media said.
The test train No 55537, which was used to test seismic equipment, collided with construction workers entering the railway line on a curve at Luoyangzhen Station in Kunming, the capital of Yunnan, according to state broadcaster CCTV.
Railway and local government authorities immediately activated their emergency response to manage rescue efforts after the incident. Station operations had since resumed, and the injured were receiving medical treatment, it said.
In a social media post, Kunming’s railway authority expressed condolence to the victims and their families. The cause of the accident is being investigated.

data.title: Hong Kong retail investors still flock to US tech stocks like Nvidia amid AI bubble fears
data.description:
Hong Kong’s retail investors are still flocking to US artificial intelligence and other tech stocks despite market volatility, but early signs of shifting sentiment have emerged, according to David Friedland, Asia-Pacific head at US firm Interactive Brokers.
Demand for US assets among Hong Kong and regional clients remained strong this year, with overnight trading in American shares doubling over the past year, driven by large-cap technology stocks and AI infrastructure, Friedland said.
“The US has effectively been on a bull run for more than 15 years,” he said in an interview with the Post. “That’s where the liquidity is, and that’s where names like Tesla and Nvidia have become high fliers and driven a lot of business.”
While early “cracks” were appearing as gains in influential stocks and bitcoin plateaued, retail behaviour was firmly in “buy the dip” mode, with US market sell-offs often met by quick inflows from individual traders, according to Friedland.

His comments come amid a sharp market pullback in recent weeks as concerns over an AI bubble have shaken investor confidence, following months of AI-driven momentum that propelled benchmark indices to new highs.
A series of prominent AI stocks, including Nvidia, saw double-digit declines in November, while broader indices such as the Nasdaq and S&P 500 retreated as much as 8.6 per cent and 5.4 per cent, respectively, from their October peaks.
Still, some investment banks remain bullish. UBS recently raised its target for the S&P 500 to 7,500 points by the end of 2026, while Deutsche Bank projected the index would reach 8,000 points next year, both attributing the growth to accelerating AI-driven corporate earnings.
When asked if the AI boom constituted a bubble, Friedland compared it to the dot-com era, in which many internet start-ups attracted fervent venture capital but struggled to achieve profitability.
“Back in the 1990s, everyone wanted to have a ‘dot-com’ in their names. A lot of those companies disappeared, but if your portfolio happened to hold Google, Microsoft or Amazon, you did incredibly well,” he said.
“We’re in a similar phase now. There’s no way every AI company will be successful 10 years from now, but those that survive will just continue to grow.”

Friedland described his outlook as “short-term slightly bearish, long-term bullish” on global markets, with AI adoption a key factor.
While US markets remained the primary focus for traders, he noted that Hong Kong was showing signs of recovery after several challenging years marked by political unrest, the Covid-19 pandemic and a dearth of new listings.
“Hong Kong looks like it has turned the corner,” Friedland said, citing the stock market rebound this year, which boosted liquidity and client engagement.
The volume of Hong Kong dollar assets held on Interactive Brokers’ platform had risen, including from overseas clients allocating capital to the city’s markets, according to Friedland.
“The amount of Hong Kong dollars we’re holding is quite large,” he said, without providing a specific figure. “It’s not just people living in Hong Kong – the global community is putting money into Hong Kong dollars.”
The firm had eliminated subscription fees for local initial public offerings in anticipation that the city’s deal pipeline would drive further growth in the sector, Friedland said.

data.title: 13 new Hong Kong restaurants to try in December 2025, from Japanese to steak
data.description:
If you are looking for an excuse to venture to different parts of the city, a new restaurant is as good a reason as any.
Among the latest openings include a Japanese kappou restaurant at The Henderson building in Central, as well as outlets offering pre- or post-event meals at the East Kowloon Cultural Centre and Kai Tak Sports Park.
Two new authentic Cantonese eateries in the style of dai pai dongs have also opened in Wan Chai.
Read on to see where to satisfy your culinary curiosity in December.
1. Hana no Kumo
Perched on the 38th floor of The Henderson in Central, Hana no Kumo offers an extraordinary Japanese kappou (a multi-course meal cooked in front of the diner) experience.
Under the guidance of executive chef Ogawa Masaru, the menu celebrates seasonal ingredients and features exquisite dishes such as steamed abalone and sea urchin with seaweed sauce, sashimi, and donabe (claypot) rice with black truffles.
Summit 38, 38/F, The Henderson, 2 Murray Road, Central
2. Torikaze

Landmark Hong Kong welcomes the arrival of yakitori restaurant Torikaze to Central.
This debut marks the first Hong Kong venture from revered master chef Yoshiteru Ikegawa of the notoriously hard-to-book Torishiki Ichimon in Tokyo.
The restaurant focuses on technical perfection, grilling humble ingredients over pure binchotan charcoal.
The menu elevates simple skewers into a sensorial encounter, where each bite, from classic chicken cuts to charred seasonal vegetables, is executed to incredible precision.
43-45/F, Forty-Five, Gloucester Tower, Landmark, 15 Queen’s Road Central
3. Petit Jardin

Petit Jardin in The Peak Tower offers a contemporary and casual take on Shanghainese and Jiangnan cuisine.
As a subsidiary of the well-known Jardin De Jade, the restaurant features creative dishes including the signature Jiangnan gua bao (literally “cut bun”) platter and the must-try Shanghai juicy trio assorted xiaolongbao.
With innovative cocktails and sweeping views of Victoria Harbour, Petit Jardin provides an elegant yet relaxed setting for enjoying a modern taste of Shanghai high above Hong Kong.
Shop 103, Level 1, The Peak Tower, 128 Peak Road, The Peak
4. Gyorai
Kumamoto’s popular tsukemen (dipping ramen) shop will open its first overseas offshoot in Wan Chai on December 3, and long queues are expected.
Must-tries include the eatery’s signature spicy miso tsukemen in a blended pork and seafood broth.
Another popular favourite is the curry tsukemen, which is cooked in the same special broth blend.
389 Lockhart Road, Wan Chai
5. Lai’s Kitchen
If you are missing the wok hei (“breath of the wok”) of stir-fries or the crunchiness of claypot rice, then head to Lai’s Kitchen on Thompson Road in Wan Chai, opened by the same team behind Flower Drum and Fat J’s Char Siu.
Dig into dishes such as typhoon shelter mantis shrimp, deep-fried squid, and hawthorn and olive spare ribs.
Crisp claypot rice is a draw here, with over a dozen to choose from such as perilla leaf, black bean and eel, or minced beef with egg.
There is something for everyone at this late-night joint.
1/F, Harvard Commercial Building, 105-111 Thomson Road, Wan Chai
6. Boat King
If you do not feel like cooking at home but are craving homestyle dishes, Boat King on Ship Street is a new option, providing fresh seafood and solid stir-fries for the whole family.
Sink your teeth into diced black pepper beef with potatoes as well as pepper pig stomach soup and soft and silky congees.
Shop 1, Pao Yip Building, 1-7 Ship Street, Wan Chai
7. Kyanbasu by Mt. Yotei
Lamb enthusiasts rejoice! Those familiar with the original Mt. Yotei lamb yakiniku (grilled meat) restaurant in Causeway Bay will be happy to find that they have opened an omakase offshoot in Central.
Feast on special menu items all made with lamb, such as skewers, char siu, dumplings, soupy curry and more.
40 Gough Street, Central
8. La Baia

La Baia is a vibrant new all-day destination bringing the comfort of Italian cuisine to the waterfront Dining Cove at Kai Tak Sports Park.
This new spot, with its stylish coastal-inspired interior and sunny alfresco patio, offers a menu showcasing house-made pastas and 24-hour fermented Neapolitan pizzas.
Guests can look forward to rich, shareable mains like herb roast chicken, or savour the decadent sea urchin aglio olio linguine, coated in a creamy umami emulsion.
DC-010, Dining Cove, Kai Tak Sports Park , Kai Tak, Kowloon.
9. Mi Manchi

Tucked away in the East Kowloon Cultural Centre, Mi Manchi is a heartfelt new Italian restaurant that offers classic dishes with a Chinese twist.
The menu weaves local ingredients into Italian classics. Alongside handmade pastas and 24-hour sourdough pizzas, dishes like the spaghetti alla red shrimp – featuring seafood marinated in huadiao wine and served tableside with an aromatic wine spray – showcase a delightful and surprising culinary perspective.
1/F, East Kowloon Cultural Centre, 60 Ngau Tau Kok Road, Kowloon
10. Zozzona

A Japanese pasta specialist has opened up in Tsim Sha Tsui.
Fresh pasta is made on the premises daily. Diners can enjoy traditional options such as linguine vongole and tagliolini freddi, or try something with a Japanese twist like Kurobuta pork meatballs.
Flat A, Quality Tower, 29-31 Hillwood Rd, Tsim Sha Tsui
11. La Vache!

Steak frites specialist La Vache! has opened its largest Hong Kong branch yet at Exchange Square.
The concept remains the same and uncomplicated: a single menu featuring a crisp organic salad followed by perfectly cooked USDA prime rib-eye steak and a bottomless supply of golden frites with signature sauce.
This new, spacious outpost also introduces a vibrant bar serving hearty snacks like the decadent steak sandwich.
Shop 103, 1 Two Exchange Square, Connaught Rd Central
12. Sushi Raku

A prominent restaurant chain in Hong Kong’s sushi scene, Sushi Raku has opened a new outlet in the dynamic BaseHall 01 food hall.
Renowned as the city’s bluefin tuna specialist, the restaurant sources directly from Tokyo’s Toyosu Market and secures the finest fish daily from legendary tuna wholesaler Yamayuki.
Diners can savour wet-aged tuna and other seasonal selections.
BaseHall 01, Shop LG9, LG/F, Jardine House, 1 Connaught Place, Central
13. Emmer Pizza Central
The acclaimed Emmer Pizza, celebrated for its artisanal crusts, is expanding its footprint with a new branch at Landmark in Central.
After winning a loyal following in Admiralty, this new location brings its signature pizzas to the heart of the city’s bustling business and shopping district.
Diners can enjoy tried-and-true favourites such as the funghi, parma and carnivore pizza, or try new creations such as Thai-flavoured pad kra pao.
Shop 217A, 2/F, Landmark Atrium, 15 Queen’s Road Central

data.title: Ex-UC Berkeley scientist dies aged 44 amid growing concern over toll on Chinese academics
data.description:
A Chinese biomedical researcher whose academic trajectory included years at the University of California, Berkeley, and collaborations with pioneering chemical engineers has died at the age of 44.
Xie Hongxue, who had been working as a lecturer for nearly five years at the school of materials science and engineering at West Anhui University, a small regional college in central China, died in Wuhan on November 16 from an unnamed illness, according to Shanghai-based news site ThePaper.cn.
Xie’s journey reflects a growing paradox in Chinese higher education. Despite elite overseas training and a prolific research record, some returnees now face diminishing prospects, ending up in under-resourced universities with heavy workloads and limited support.
He is among the dozens of younger Chinese academics who have died this year, an issue that has raised further questions about the country’s cutthroat academic system.
He spent several years at Berkeley, studying under John Prausnitz, who is considered the father of molecular thermodynamics in chemical engineering.
His work helped make modern chemical manufacturing processes more energy-efficient, safer and less polluting, according to the American Academy of Arts and Sciences.
Xie’s research focus was on biochemistry and the green chemical industry. At the time of his death, he was working on four research projects, according to ThePaper.
He published more than 10 articles in leading journals, was granted seven invention patents and 30 utility model patents and co-authored two books, according to his university profile.
His published works covered the use of plant species to improve chemical processes and the development of nanoparticles and nanocomposites. The patents included a device to separate and purify animal serum proteins and an adeno-associated virus purification kit.
In May, Xie helped lead a team from West Anhui University to the Chinese vocational education innovation and entrepreneurship competition, where they won first place in the undergraduate group for their work on automated DNA sequencing, according to the university.
Xie obtained his undergraduate and master’s degrees at Henan University of Technology from 2000 to 2007, after which he completed his doctoral studies at the China University of Geosciences in Wuhan in 2011.
Between 2009 and 2011, he studied at Berkeley through a joint doctoral training programme before staying on as a postdoctoral researcher until 2014.
Xie also worked as a research scientist at US biotechnology company Alstem and helped found the Chinese branch of Quintara Biosciences.


In 2019, Xie joined Xinjiang Normal University’s school of chemical engineering as a part-time lecturer.
In June 2020, he joined West Anhui University, one of the country’s lesser-known universities, as a full-time lecturer.
While working there, he also stayed involved in the commercial sector, with roles at Anhui Dezhengtang Pharmaceutical Technology Group and as the chief scientist of the packaging company Ningbo Joy Intelligent Logistics Technology.
Xie was the recipient of a scholarship from the China Scholarship Council for overseas study, received a doctoral talent allowance from the city of Wuhan, and was selected for a municipal talent programme. He also helped to lead a provincial gene detection platform project in Hubei, according to his university profile.
Xie also served as the founding president of the Wuhan chapter of the Berkeley Chinese Alumni International Association.
In a memorial posted on social media last week, the association said he was dedicated to growing the alumni network, which included social events, exchanges and support for younger alumni.
“His dedication to alumni affairs, his commitment to serving alumni, and his steadfast commitment to unity and mutual assistance will continue to inspire every one of us to move forward firmly along the path he left unfinished,” the memorial said.
Passionate about science? Dive deeper with the Dark Matters newsletter, a weekly in-depth analysis on China’s rise in science, technology and military that goes beneath the surface. Sign up for free now.

data.title: As Philippines targets more Pogo figures, can Marcos regain public trust?
data.description:
A travel ban imposed by the Philippines against two figures tied to its notorious offshore gaming industry (Pogos) marks a bid by the Ferdinand Marcos Jnr administration to boost public standing, but the country’s unfolding flood-control scandal remains the biggest mover of political fortunes.
On Tuesday, the Department of Foreign Affairs confirmed that it had cancelled the passports of Harry Roque, a former spokesman for ex-president Rodrigo Duterte, and Cassandra Li Ong, an associate of former mayor Alice Guo.
The move came just days after Guo, a central figure linked to the Pogos sector, was sentenced to life imprisonment for human trafficking.
While Filipinos are likely to support further moves to target individuals linked to Pogos, they are more concerned about the huge sums purportedly siphoned from flood-control projects, analysts say.
Roque and Ong face trafficking charges in connection with Lucky South 99, a Pogos-linked compound, where authorities rescued nearly 200 workers in a raid last year and discovered evidence of torture, human trafficking and scam operations.
The raid took place amid a year-long government crackdown on the now-prohibited Pogos, which flourished during the previous Duterte administration.
The cancellation of passports belonging to Roque and Ong happened seven months after charges were filed against them and several days after a Philippine court sentenced Guo and seven others to life imprisonment for human trafficking, and ordered the Baofu compound in Bamban, Tarlac, to be forfeited to the government.


Ong is the authorised representative of Lucky South 99, while Roque acted as the company’s lawyer during its application for a government licence.
In charges filed against Roque last year, authorities alleged that he had taken part in “illegal activities” involving the company. Roque is reportedly seeking asylum at The Hague in the Netherlands, where he claims that he is being targeted for political persecution by Manila due to his ties with Duterte.
Ong has not been seen after her release in December from detention for contempt during a Senate hearing. The Department of Justice has offered a reward of 1 million pesos (US$17,000) for credible information on Ong’s whereabouts. The Philippine Anti-Organised Crime Commission traced her last known location to Japan in January.
“Cassie Li Ong and Harry Roque are facing serious charges of human trafficking. It is only right that their passports were cancelled, especially since they appear to be evading the law,” opposition senator Risa Hontiveros, who led investigations by a committee into alleged human trafficking by Pogos last year, said in a statement earlier this week.
“Our government must also not stop until other criminal syndicates that enabled these Pogo scam hubs are held accountable. The conviction of Alice Guo is not the end of the story. She is not the only one who must answer for these crimes – everyone involved should be held responsible.”

Flood control scandal dominates
Arjan Aguirre, an assistant professor at the Ateneo de Manila University’s political science department, said the court ruling in Guo’s case and the passport cancellation announcement were a boost to the Marcos administration in showing that it was producing “concrete results” in the crackdown against Pogos.
“These actions appear interconnected and consistent with the administration’s broader effort to project firmness and restore public confidence,” Aguirre told This Week in Asia.
Coupled with the nationwide investigations against individuals allegedly involved in the flood-control scandal, Marcos is determined to bolster public trust in his administration through his crackdown against crime and corruption, according to Aguirre.
A Pulse Asia survey in September showed that the president’s approval rating fell to 33 per cent in September from 42 per cent in June. In comparison, the rating for Vice-President Sara Duterte-Carpio fell to 55 per cent from 59 per cent over the same period.
Aguirre said the Marcos administration’s anti-corruption moves were also aimed at preventing a deterioration in the political climate to an extent that Duterte could stage a comeback.
Marcos’ approval ratings are unlikely to improve in any significant way
Arjan Aguirre, Ateneo de Manila University’s academic
Matthew David Ordonez, a political science lecturer at De La Salle University, said the actions taken against individuals linked to Pogos were long overdue following a promise made by Marcos in his State of the Nation Address last year.
“Perhaps this more aggressive turn on the Pogos can offset the flood control scandal and preserve what remaining approval the administration has among the people while reminding the people of Duterte’s role in bringing Pogos in,” Ordonez said.
However, he noted that the crackdown against Pogos had adversely affected sectors such as property and retail.
Aguirre said that the trillions of pesos lost to corruption arising from the flood-control scandal would continue to overshadow any further moves against individuals linked to Pogos.
“Even if this leads to Roque’s arrest, it won’t divert the public from the core issue – the flood-control scandal. Marcos’ approval ratings are unlikely to improve in any significant way.”

data.title: Oxfam Trailwaker cancelled, questions over Cyclothon as Hong Kong reels from fire deaths
data.description:
The 2025 Oxfam Trailwalker race that was expected to start on Friday, has reportedly been cancelled.
Organisers held a meeting this morning, and decided not to go ahead in light of the events in Tai Po, sources say.
Official confirmation is expected soon, with almost 4,000 runners, some who have travelled from overseas, waiting for news.
Separately, organisers of Sunday’s Cyclothon were also meeting on Thursday to decide if that event should also be delayed.
More to follow...
